content.text: Voice latency is the end-to-end delay between the moment a user finishes speaking and the moment the voice agent begins playing its spoken response. In a real-time voice agent system, several processing stages execute in sequence: voice activity detection (VAD), speech-to-text transcription (STT), large-language-model (LLM) inference, text-to-speech synthesis (TTS), and network transport. Each stage adds a measurable delay, and their sum determines whether a conversation feels natural or broken.
The primary metric practitioners track is

: the interval from the end of the user's speech to the first audio sample arriving at the speaker. TTFB is distinct from total latency — which includes the full duration of the synthesized response — but it is TTFB that governs perceived responsiveness. Users notice the gap before the agent starts speaking, not how long it takes to finish a sentence.
Typical per-stage ranges on a single-region cloud deployment with a 50 ms network one-way:
20–50 ms — a lightweight on-device or gateway model monitors for silence.
200–800 ms — shorter with streaming models that return partial results.
200–1 000 ms — the dominant bottleneck in most architectures.
100–300 ms — triggered on the first complete sentence from the LLM.
A well-optimized pipeline targeting a sub-second TTFB must shave time from each of these stages rather than optimizing just one.

Human conversation operates within tight latency norms. Psycholinguistic research shows that gaps in spoken dialogue are typically 200–300 ms; any pause above 500 ms is perceived as unusual, and pauses above 1–2 seconds prompt speakers to fill the gap or assume the listener has disengaged. Voice assistants are held to a stricter standard than web applications: a user tolerates a two-second page load as background latency, but will interrupt, repeat themselves, or abandon a call if the agent takes three seconds to begin its response.
Three hard perception thresholds define the experience space:
the response feels immediate — conversational and natural, indistinguishable from a human pause.
noticeable but acceptable — comparable to a mildly delayed mobile call; task completion rates remain high.
clearly slow; users may start speaking again or express frustration.
conversation flow breaks; users assume the system is stuck, generating re-prompts and call abandonment.

Because users cannot see a progress indicator, latency is the only signal they have about system health. High latency also creates barge-in problems: users start speaking before the agent finishes responding, causing overlapping speech and corrupted turns. In customer service deployments, every 100 ms of added TTFB measurably reduces task completion rates and first-call resolution scores.
sub-500 ms TTFB matches human turn-taking cadence, reducing friction in every interaction.
faster responses leave less silence for users to fill with repeated questions.
customer service metrics consistently improve when TTFB drops below 1 000 ms.
Reduced cost when combined with model right-sizing:
a faster, smaller model can outperform a slower, larger one on user satisfaction metrics at lower inference cost per call.
shorter per-turn latency makes barge-in detection easier — the gap between the user speaking and the agent reacting is smaller.
low-latency pipelines can support mid-sentence barge-in, letting users redirect the agent without waiting for the full response.
for consumer voice products, latency is a primary differentiator that users directly perceive and compare.

The fastest route to lower latency is a smaller LLM. A 7 B parameter open-source model can return its first token in under 100 ms on a modern GPU, versus 300–800 ms for a large frontier model. But smaller models produce shorter, less nuanced answers and may fail on complex tasks. Every latency–quality trade-off must be measured against the actual use case, not assumed.
Streaming TTS on the first complete sentence is the largest single latency win but carries its own risks:
If the LLM's first sentence is grammatically incomplete when TTS fires — for example, a clause that needs a second clause to make sense — the synthesized audio can sound cut off or misleading.

Once audio has been dispatched to the TTS service, it cannot be recalled. If the LLM changes direction in the second sentence, the first words have already been synthesized and may contradict what follows.
Sentence-boundary detection on raw streaming tokens is heuristic and can misfire on abbreviations, decimal numbers, or ellipses.
eliminates latency for deterministic responses but requires a content catalog and cache invalidation logic; cached audio becomes stale if the script changes.
reduces network RTT from 50–200 ms to 5–20 ms but multiplies operational complexity across multiple regions.
lets the LLM start processing before the user finishes speaking, saving 100–400 ms, but risks sending an incomplete transcript to the LLM if the partial is acted on too early.
eliminates per-turn TLS overhead but requires connection management and monitoring for stale connections.
Each stage in the pipeline adds latency through a different mechanism. Understanding the source of delay in each stage is the prerequisite for reducing it.

A lightweight model (Silero VAD, WebRTC VAD) runs in real time on the client or edge gateway and monitors the audio stream. It fires an end-of-speech event after detecting a silence longer than a configurable threshold — typically 200–400 ms. The silence duration is added directly to every turn: a threshold of 400 ms means every user response has at least 400 ms of pipeline dead time before STT begins. Shorter thresholds reduce latency but increase false-positive triggers that cut off mid-sentence pauses.

The audio buffer is transmitted to a transcription service. Cloud STT APIs (Deepgram, Google Speech-to-Text, AssemblyAI) return results in 200–800 ms depending on audio duration, model size, and whether streaming mode is enabled. In

, partial transcripts are returned incrementally as the user speaks, enabling the agent to begin forming a response before the user finishes — but at the risk of acting on an incomplete sentence. For best accuracy, most production systems wait for the VAD end-of-speech event and then send the full audio in a single request.
The transcript is appended to the conversation history and sent to the LLM. TTFT — the interval from dispatching the request to receiving the first output token — is governed by: (1)
(larger models require more compute per forward pass); (2)
(the KV-cache prefill step is O(n) in token count — a 4 000-token system prompt can add 200–600 ms versus a 500-token prompt on the same hardware); (3)

Once a complete sentence is buffered from the LLM token stream, it is dispatched to a TTS service (ElevenLabs, Cartesia, OpenAI TTS). Streaming TTS APIs return the first audio chunk within 80–300 ms of receiving the text. The key optimization is firing TTS as soon as the first sentence boundary is detected rather than accumulating the full LLM response — this overlaps TTS synthesis with ongoing LLM generation and reduces TTFB by hundreds of milliseconds.

Each cross-service API call incurs a network round-trip. A client in Western Europe calling a US-East-hosted STT service adds ~90 ms one-way. HTTP/1.1 adds a TLS handshake on every new TCP connection (30–100 ms); HTTP/2 with keepalive eliminates this overhead on subsequent turns within the same session. WebSocket connections established at the start of a call avoid both the TCP three-way handshake and TLS negotiation on every turn.

attrs.alt: Voice agent latency pipeline diagram showing sequential stages: VAD (20–50ms, amber), STT (200–800ms, blue), network (20–100ms, slate), LLM TTFT (200–1000ms, purple), TTS first chunk (100–300ms, green), and final network delivery. Human perception thresholds are shown: under 300ms feels instant, over 2s breaks flow. Key optimisations are listed: stream TTS on first sentence, parallel STT and context retrieval, edge deployment, pre-cache common responses, HTTP/2 keepalive.

content.text: The diagram shows each processing stage as a proportionally-sized colored segment. The pipeline runs left to right: the user stops speaking, VAD fires, STT transcribes the audio, the transcript travels over the network to the LLM, the LLM produces its first token, the TTS service synthesizes the first sentence, and the audio travels back to the client. The event dots on the timeline baseline mark the boundary between stages — each is a measurable timestamp in an instrumented implementation.

The LLM TTFT segment (purple) is the widest variable contributor in most production systems. Unlike STT or TTS, it cannot be overlapped with other work without fundamentally changing the architecture (e.g. speculative prefetching). The TTS segment (green) is deliberately positioned early — it begins as soon as the first complete sentence exits the LLM, not after full generation, which is the most impactful single optimization available.
Aggressive latency optimization is mandatory for any voice interface where conversational flow is the core experience. The principal deployment contexts are:
customer service agents handling phone calls where users have zero tolerance for silence gaps.
embedded in consumer devices (speakers, earbuds) where the interaction model mimics hands-free conversation.
where both the source speaker and the listener are waiting for the translated output.
replacing legacy touch-tone menus where users expect phone-call pacing.
Latency optimization is less critical for:
Asynchronous transcription (voice memos, meeting recordings) where the user is not waiting for a real-time response.
Batch voice-to-text pipelines (podcast transcription, accessibility captioning after the fact).
Voice interface prototypes or internal demos where user expectation is set by the demo context.
A structured approach prevents premature optimization. Measure first, optimize the bottleneck, then re-measure before moving to the next stage.
Add per-stage timestamp instrumentation (see Examples) and collect P50 / P99 TTFB for your current baseline over at least 100 real turns.
Identify the dominant bottleneck. Typical findings: LLM TTFT accounts for >50% of total TTFB in most first-generation deployments; STT is the second-most-common bottleneck.
Apply streaming TTS on first sentence before anything else — this is the highest leverage change with the lowest risk and requires no infrastructure change.
Move STT and any context retrieval to run in parallel using asyncio.gather() or equivalent concurrency primitives.
Audit your system prompt length. Reduce it to the minimum needed for the task. Every 1 000 unnecessary tokens adds 50–200 ms to LLM TTFT.
Enable HTTP/2 or WebSocket keepalive connections to STT, LLM, and TTS services. Reuse connections across turns within the same session.
Evaluate whether a smaller LLM meets quality requirements for your task. Run an offline A/B quality eval before switching.
If network RTT exceeds 50 ms one-way, evaluate regional or edge deployment of at least the LLM inference endpoint.
Pre-synthesize deterministic responses (greetings, hold messages, common FAQs) at server startup and serve them from a local cache.
Re-measure P50 / P99 after each change. Some optimizations have unexpected compound effects; some do not move the needle at all in your specific workload.
dataclass that captures timestamps at each pipeline stage boundary. Call
after each turn and write the result to your observability store to build P50 / P99 dashboards per stage.

import time
import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass

import anthropic


@dataclass
class LatencyBreakdown:
    """Per-stage timing for one complete voice turn."""
    vad_end_ts: float = 0.0          # VAD fired — user finished speaking
    stt_complete_ts: float = 0.0     # STT returned transcript
    llm_request_ts: float = 0.0      # LLM request dispatched
    llm_ttft_ts: float = 0.0         # first LLM token received
    tts_request_ts: float = 0.0      # TTS called with first complete sentence
    tts_chunk_ts: float = 0.0        # TTS returned first audio chunk
    audio_play_ts: float = 0.0       # audio playback started

    @property
    def stt_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.stt_complete_ts - self.vad_end_ts) * 1000

    @property
    def llm_ttft_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.llm_ttft_ts - self.llm_request_ts) * 1000

    @property
    def tts_first_chunk_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.tts_chunk_ts - self.tts_request_ts) * 1000

    @property
    def end_to_end_ms(self) -> float:
        """TTFB: VAD trigger → first audio byte."""
        return (self.audio_play_ts - self.vad_end_ts) * 1000

    def report(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "stt_ms":            round(self.stt_ms, 1),
            "llm_ttft_ms":       round(self.llm_ttft_ms, 1),
            "tts_first_chunk_ms": round(self.tts_first_chunk_ms, 1),
            "end_to_end_ms":     round(self.end_to_end_ms, 1),
        }


async def instrumented_voice_turn(
    audio_bytes: bytes,
    stt_client,
    llm_client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    tts_client,
    history: list[dict],
) -> tuple[bytes, LatencyBreakdown]:
    """
    One complete voice turn with per-stage timestamps.

    Returns the first audio chunk and a filled LatencyBreakdown.
    Log breakdown.report() to a time-series store (e.g. Prometheus,
    Datadog, or a Postgres table) on every turn to track P50 / P99.
    """
    lat = LatencyBreakdown()
    lat.vad_end_ts = time.monotonic()
# ... (truncated for brevity)
Async Parallel Processing and Sentence-Boundary TTS Streaming

The following example shows two patterns that compound to reduce TTFB: (1) parallelizing STT and context retrieval so retrieval is not on the critical path, and (2) streaming TTS sentence-by-sentence from the LLM token stream.

import asyncio
from typing import AsyncIterator

import anthropic


async def parallel_stt_and_context(
    audio_bytes: bytes,
    stt_client,
    retriever,
    lookahead_query: str,
) -> tuple[str, list[str]]:
    """
    Run STT transcription and context retrieval concurrently.

    While waiting for the STT result we issue a retrieval query based on
    the previous turn's text (a lookahead heuristic). In practice this
    removes 50–200 ms of retrieval time from the critical path because
    both operations complete before either result is needed downstream.
    """
    stt_task = asyncio.create_task(stt_client.transcribe(audio_bytes))
    retrieval_task = asyncio.create_task(
        retriever.search(lookahead_query, top_k=3)
    )
    transcript, docs = await asyncio.gather(stt_task, retrieval_task)
    return transcript, docs


async def stream_tts_sentence_by_sentence(
    llm_client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic,
    messages: list[dict],
    tts_client,
) -> AsyncIterator[bytes]:
    """
    Consume the LLM token stream and yield TTS audio chunks as soon as
    each complete sentence is available.

    The first audio chunk is produced within (LLM TTFT + TTS latency)
    rather than waiting for the full generation — typically saving 500 ms
    to 2 s of perceived latency.
    """
    buffer = ""
    SENTENCE_ENDS = {'.', '!', '?'}

    async with llm_client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=messages,
    ) as stream:
        async for chunk in stream.text_stream:
            buffer += chunk

            # Find the last clean sentence boundary in the buffer
            last_end = -1
            for i in range(len(buffer) - 1, -1, -1):
                if buffer[i] in SENTENCE_ENDS:
                    # Avoid splitting mid-abbreviation: require trailing space
                    if i + 1 < len(buffer) and buffer[i + 1] == ' ':
                        last_end = i
                        break

# ... (truncated for brevity)
Warm-up Caching for Cold-Start and Common Responses

Cold-start latency — the first request to a freshly scaled inference server — can be 3–10× longer than steady state. Pre-synthesizing common TTS phrases and warming up the LLM endpoint at server startup eliminates both classes of spike.

# Warm up inference endpoints and pre-cache common TTS responses
# to eliminate cold-start and greeting latency spikes.

import asyncio
import hashlib
from pathlib import Path

import anthropic

CACHE_DIR = Path("/tmp/tts_cache")
CACHE_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

COMMON_PHRASES = [
    "Hello! How can I help you today?",
    "I'm looking that up for you now.",
    "Could you please repeat that?",
    "Thank you for calling. Have a great day!",
]


def cache_key(text: str) -> str:
    return hashlib.sha256(text.encode()).hexdigest()[:16]


async def warm_tts_cache(tts_client) -> None:
    """
    Pre-synthesize common phrases at startup.
    Subsequent calls to get_cached_audio() return instantly (<1 ms).
    """
    tasks = []
    for phrase in COMMON_PHRASES:
        key = cache_key(phrase)
        path = CACHE_DIR / f"{key}.wav"
        if not path.exists():
            tasks.append(_synthesize_and_cache(tts_client, phrase, path))
    if tasks:
        await asyncio.gather(*tasks)


async def _synthesize_and_cache(tts_client, text: str, path: Path) -> None:
    audio = await tts_client.synthesize(text)
    path.write_bytes(audio)


def get_cached_audio(text: str) -> bytes | None:
    """Return cached audio bytes or None if not cached."""
    path = CACHE_DIR / f"{cache_key(text)}.wav"
    return path.read_bytes() if path.exists() else None


async def warm_llm_endpoint(llm_client: anthropic.AsyncAnthropic) -> None:
    """
    Send a minimal prompt to force the LLM server to load model weights
    into GPU memory before the first real call.  Cold-start TTFT can be
    3–10× longer than steady-state.
    """
    await llm_client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=1,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}],
    )

A customer service voice agent for a European telecom provider was measured at 2.8 s median TTFB. Stage-level instrumentation revealed the breakdown: STT (Whisper large-v3) contributed 420 ms, network 90 ms each way, LLM TTFT 1 800 ms (a large frontier model on a US-East endpoint with a 4 000-token system prompt), TTS 300 ms (full generation before synthesis).
Five targeted changes were applied in order:
Replaced Whisper large-v3 with Deepgram Nova-2 streaming:
STT dropped from 420 ms to 220 ms. The streaming API also enabled partial transcripts, reducing the effective STT-to-LLM handoff time.
Compressed system prompt from 4 000 to 800 tokens:
LLM TTFT dropped from 1 800 ms to 720 ms. The prompt was rewritten to use structured examples rather than long prose instructions.
Enabled streaming TTS on first sentence:

TTS latency as perceived by the user dropped to ~180 ms (the time to synthesize the first sentence) rather than 300 ms (the time to synthesize the full response). This change alone saved 120 ms of TTFB and 600–1 200 ms of total response start delay on longer answers.
Added HTTP/2 keepalive on all service connections:
saved 75 ms per turn by eliminating per-turn TLS negotiation overhead.
Moved LLM inference endpoint to EU-West:
80% of callers were in Western Europe. Network RTT dropped from 90 ms to 15 ms each way, saving 150 ms per turn.

Result: median TTFB fell from 2.8 s to 0.88 s — well below the 1 000 ms "feels fast" threshold. Task completion rates improved by 14% and call abandonment dropped by 22% within the first two weeks, without any change to the LLM model or response quality.

The most common mistake is optimizing the stage that seems slowest rather than measuring which stage is actually slowest. In the real-world example above, the developers assumed STT was the bottleneck (because Whisper is known to be slow) when in fact LLM TTFT from the oversized system prompt was the dominant contributor. Always capture per-stage timestamps for at least 50 turns before making changes.
Waiting for Full LLM Output Before Calling TTS
This is the single most common pipeline design error. Developers call the LLM without streaming, wait for the

, then send the entire response text to TTS. This adds the full generation time — often 1–3 s on a medium-length response — to the TTFB, when the first sentence is available within 200–400 ms of TTFT. Always stream the LLM and fire TTS sentence-by-sentence.

Whisper large-v3 is accurate but 3× slower than Whisper small for the same audio. For typical customer service audio — clean English, phone-call quality, domain-constrained vocabulary — a smaller streaming STT model achieves near-equal word-error rate at a fraction of the latency. Profile STT accuracy versus latency on your actual audio before choosing a model.

A silence threshold that is too short (< 200 ms) triggers end-of-speech on natural mid-sentence pauses, sending incomplete transcripts to the LLM. A threshold that is too long (> 600 ms) adds that full duration to every turn as unavoidable dead time. Calibrate the threshold on recordings from your actual user population; English typically works well at 300–400 ms, other languages may need different values.

Each call to an HTTPS STT, LLM, or TTS endpoint over a new TCP connection incurs a TLS handshake costing 30–100 ms. In a voice session with 10 turns, this adds 300 ms to 1 s of avoidable overhead. Use HTTP/2 with keepalive or WebSocket connections established once per session.

Auto-scaling inference servers may spin down between low-traffic periods. The first request to a cold server can take 3–10× longer than steady-state TTFT. Implement keep-warm pinging (a lightweight request every 30–60 s) for latency-sensitive endpoints, and set your minimum replica count to 1.
Instrument every stage with monotonic timestamps.

Log per-stage breakdowns to a time-series store (Prometheus, Datadog, InfluxDB). Alert on P99 spikes, not just median. Median P50 can look healthy while P99 is breaking the conversation for 1 in 100 callers.
Always stream TTS from first sentence.

This is the highest-leverage optimization in virtually every pipeline. Fire TTS as soon as the first sentence-ending punctuation (.!?) appears in the LLM token stream with a trailing space or end-of-string.
to run transcription and any vector search or database lookup concurrently, removing retrieval latency from the critical path.
Keep system prompts under 1 000 tokens for latency-critical paths.
Each additional 1 000 tokens adds 50–200 ms to LLM TTFT through KV-cache prefill overhead. Store long instructions in retrieval or use prompt caching.
Use the smallest LLM that meets quality requirements for the task.

Benchmark a smaller model on real call samples before dismissing it. A 8 B model with a 600 ms TTFT may produce better user experience than a 70 B model with a 1 400 ms TTFT even if raw response quality scores are lower.
Example: STT < 400 ms P99, LLM TTFT < 600 ms P99, TTS first chunk < 250 ms P99. Treat a broken SLO as an incident, not a normal variance.
Pre-cache synthesized audio for deterministic responses.
Greetings, hold messages, and FAQ answers that never change can be synthesized at server startup and served from memory with zero synthesis latency.
Use HTTP/2 keepalive or WebSocket connections within a session.
Establish the connection once per call and reuse it for all turns.
Send a minimal probe request at startup and at regular intervals to prevent cold-start latency spikes on the first real call after idle periods.
Best-in-class real-time latency; streaming partial transcripts available
High accuracy; use small/medium models for lower latency
Good multilingual support; streaming mode required for low latency
Fastest available streaming TTS as of 2025
High voice quality; streaming API essential
Wide language support; low latency on streaming endpoint
Lightweight ONNX model; runs in browser or edge gateway
C library with Python bindings; very low CPU usage
Fastest Claude model; optimized for interactive workloads
Strong quality-to-speed ratio for voice agents
Time-series metric collection; alert on P99 TTFB thresholds
Fully sequential — wait for full LLM output before TTS
Stream TTS from first sentence (recommended baseline)
Parallel STT + context retrieval + sentence TTS
On-device STT + local LLM + local TTS
Ultra-low-latency demos; requires careful partial handling

Voice agent pipelines handle audio streams that contain biometric and personally identifiable information. Latency optimizations that introduce new data paths must be reviewed for security implications.
Protect audio streams as sensitive data.
Raw audio contains voice biometrics. Log transcript hashes for debugging, not raw audio. Apply data-at-rest encryption to any audio buffered on intermediate nodes.
Enforce TLS on every service call.
STT, LLM, and TTS endpoints must be called over HTTPS/TLS 1.2+. Do not allow HTTP fallback in any environment, including development.

A cache of pre-synthesized phrases is essentially a voice script for the agent. Protect the cache storage with access controls; an attacker who can inject entries can make the agent speak arbitrary content.
Long-lived HTTP/2 connections must be periodically recycled to prevent session token reuse. Implement a maximum connection age (e.g., 30 minutes) and re-establish connections transparently.

LLM output that passes through to TTS without validation can contain profanity, sensitive information, or prompt-injection artifacts synthesized into the caller's audio. Filter LLM output for content policy compliance before dispatching to TTS.

If STT exceeds 3 s, emit a polite audio prompt ("I'm having trouble hearing you — could you repeat that?") rather than silently timing out. If LLM TTFT exceeds your SLO, play a hold phrase and retry rather than leaving the caller in silence.

In streaming STT deployments, an attacker who can inject audio into the stream before VAD fires end-of-speech can alter the transcript. Validate transcript content before passing to the LLM, especially in IVR flows that accept numeric inputs or trigger downstream actions.

The voice agent pipeline has seven measurable stages — VAD, STT, network, LLM TTFT, LLM generation, TTS, network — and each contributes independently to end-to-end latency. Measure all of them before optimizing any one.
The primary user-facing metric is time-to-first-byte (TTFB) of audio, not total response duration. Users notice the gap before the agent starts speaking; the length of the response matters far less.
Human perception thresholds are hard boundaries: under 300 ms feels instant, 300 ms – 1 s feels fast, over 2 s breaks conversational flow and triggers abandonment.
Firing TTS on the first complete sentence from the LLM token stream — rather than waiting for the full response — is the single highest-leverage optimization in most pipelines. Implement
Running STT and context retrieval in parallel removes retrieval latency from the critical path with minimal code change using asyncio.gather().
System prompt length directly taxes LLM TTFT through KV-cache prefill. Every unnecessary 1 000 tokens costs 50–200 ms; audit and trim prompts before switching inference hardware.
HTTP/2 keepalive connections eliminate per-turn TLS overhead (30–100 ms each). Establish connections once per session, not once per turn.
Always monitor P99 latency alongside P50. A tail-latency spike that affects 1% of calls still harms thousands of users per day at production scale.
Pre-synthesize deterministic phrases at server startup to achieve sub-50 ms TTFB for greetings and common hold messages — the most perceptible turns in any voice interaction.
